author: @SuperChromatic
text:
I am in the process of making a solar thermal system for my home. You video on ,making your own heat pipes was useful, but not as useful as the addition of graphene.

Which, do you think, would be the ideal medium to mix with the graphene within the heat tube?

How much graphene to solution for a 1500 mm evacuated tube? 

Can  you propose a much better heat transfer system from the "bulb" to the carrier fluid?

Where is the cheapest place to buy sufficient graphene for 20 units?

I would really appreciate your opinions.

Regards

Daniel
Isle of Anglesey.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Daniel Phipps Hiya mate I am afraid i don't have much advice to give as all this stuff is experimental and there are  no clear cut answers just as there is no 'cheap' graphene just right now. the cheapest is just to make it yourself and experiment

replies.author: @SuperChromatic
replies.text:
+Thanks for replying Robert,
I will certainly make my own Graphene as you suggest. What percentage of fluid to graphene would you mix as a starting point and is water the best fluid, from your experience?
Cheers Robert

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Daniel Phipps I would start with 0.5% by weight addition mate and take it from there

author: @BioDynamicIndustries
text: I can independently confirm Robert's claims about this fluid. I made graphene nanofluid from exactly 20 grams of high purity 325 mesh graphite and mixed it into the water loop of a computer cooling system (approx. 20 grams into 20 gallons) to observe its effects. I can report with high confidence that the temps dropped significantly. (in degrees F) CPU before: 82 avg w/ 76 min ~ to ~ 66 avg w/ 64 min | Additionally the GPU went from 115 avg & 114 min ~~ to 99 avg & 97 min. Both measured on Linux with Psensor.

replies.author: @BioDynamicIndustries
replies.text: I roughly guesstimate off the top of my head that the thermal conductivity of the fluid may perhaps be somewhere above pure silver but less than impure diamond. I'll report back after I do some analysis at the university lab and get some data on the matter.

replies.author: @BioDynamicIndustries
replies.text:
Sean LutzPicture of my results. 

http://i.imgur.com/gtpsCpI.jpg

author: @actudoran
text:
hi mate!
so effectively by addinh graphene to water you lower the specific heat of the aggregate sollution or not?

i am asking coz i might lower the cost of the heat exchangers in the plant by using such a mixture .... now graphene being tiny i doubbt willl generate any problems with the pumps ... but will look into it.

as for the exhaust gases exchanger i need the opposite to highten the specific heat .... the only way i know is making a water sollution with nh3 ... but that corrosive ... the other fluid with loads of h bonds in h3o .... :)  cannot find any info on specific heat and structural stability at above 130 degrees C ..

soŕry for typos ... android today

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't think so mate - I think it improves efficiency of thermal transfer - it's kind of like running it through a heat exchanger with nanoscale pipes - imagine a radiator where the water papes were in the nanoscale - it would cool down very quickly only because the surface area was so much greater - I think the same thing is happening here.

replies.author: @actudoran
replies.text: Aha ... I see ... so stil 4185 specific heat ...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: actudoran it will be changed a bit - it has to be - after all we have changed the system components - but to get it we'd have to measure it. Not that difficult to do - but equally not something I am likely to do anytime soon

replies.author: @actudoran
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith lol ... didn't ask for specifics since they would vary with the amount of graphene in the system ... 
Wouldn't the graphene sink to the bottom ? Any other substances needed to prevent that?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: actudoran lol - I didn't think you did mate - I was saying I wasn't going to measure the specific heat anytime soon - I probably will have to at some stage or other - but right now it's just not a thing i need to do. It is electrostearically stabalised.

Brilliant work by the way.

author: @roidroid
text:
Confusing. 
Heat pipes work via evaporation & condensation, why would graphene effect that? 
Does graphene evaporate?  (i didn't think it did). 
Does graphene effect the ability of water to evaporate?  Perhaps effecting it's vapor pressure, like dissolved salt can?

Could it be that these results are entirely from the liquid pool at the bottom of the heatpipe being more efficient at moving heat to it's liquid surface?  ie: the results would be purely from graphene's enhancing the convection within the liquid pool at the base, but would be entirely limited to that zone with no effect on the rest of the tube (ie: no effect on the cool condensation end).

I thought i understood heatpipes, as i've been idly pondering howto make *flexible* heatpipe materials for years.  These results are weird.  What do you think is going on here Rob?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think the graphene improves thermal transfer within the fluid - but Kimberley has a really good grasp of this - have a look at her comments as it is a very good explanation of what is going on - I got hold of it when she compared it to a capacitor for me

author: @KimberlyRPeacock
text: The next step would be to take two chilled water baths with a known quantity of ice and water and have it insulated, and measure both the ambient temp and in one we can calculate the input of energy from the room and in the other we can connect a heat pipe and a heat source and calculate the speed of thermal input into the water bath as a function of the temperature rise and the heat capacitance, phase change, etc.  This will give us throughput or heat flow.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: my next step is to do a bit of optimisation on the fluid - this is just the first fluid i put together - it is - of course - not just graphene in water - there's a bit more too it. There are 5 major components and they will have an effect when varied - i'll be checking that out for a bit I think - but I am going to use this simple set up as a benchmark then do what you say

replies.author: @AaronHarper
replies.text: That is a massive difference in thermal transfer!  In terms of BTUs/min, it represents a powerful application in removing heat for liquid cooled processor applications.

replies.author: @KimberlyRPeacock
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith The advantage of the thermal transfer fluid is that with the heat pipe you can on sink and source expand the surface area  and improve thermal diffusion to the heat pipes. It also acts as a thermal capacitor which is variable based on the ratio of the constituents of the fluids.  Like Fins on a conventional heat exchanger but with better dynamics.  I also think that using microwave heat input for microfluidic heat transfer with this sort of thing can yield impressive results.  Both in terms of time and energy input to achieve result.   Without giving too much away, in thermal driven sorbent systems, you will find that a stepwise function as oppose to sinusoidal signal is what you want.  At the beginning and end of each cycle fluid flow stops and we just impulse the energy to raise the temperature of the material, and then let entropy take us down hill.  Which is why microwave to susceptor input is interesting.  The susceptors absorb and radiate based upon their distribution within the fluid.  It also ties in with thermo acoustics at this point.  Google Saser Penn State.  To get an idea of what I am talking about.  You do not need the stack as the fluid itself enhanced water organises into the stack.  You take the ultrasound signal and convert it to electricity.  University of Utah a few years back did a lot of work on this.

replies.author: @AaronHarper
replies.text: Kimberly Peacock that would explain the hysteresis and delay in the transfer.

replies.author: @KimberlyRPeacock
replies.text:
Yes well the fluid has capacitance and it is not phase changing, but this can be of an advantage. Think of it like voltage which is fluctuating but you want a nice signal at 12 volts, the thermal capacitance can help keep temperature range in the sweet spot for the heat pipe while source and sink fluctuate a little.

The killer application though is when you are not phase changing but need enhanced thermal transfer and heat diffusion to separate into thermal layers or enhanced convection.   You have lowered the cost of having a massive heat collection apparatus for low temperature differential engines.

Also think of clothing with an absorbent and this system where movement pumps the fluid and provides a the vacuum for a sorbent cooler.  For runners, firefighters, people who work in elevated temperature environments.
